rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE D J H DATED JULY 24, 1990 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer To Second Amended Probate Code Sec. 850 Petition (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 The demurrers are overruled as to the Eighth and Ninth causes of action. While it is correct that these causes of action only assert forms of relief, they are forms of relief that could possibly be available on the remaining claims, and there is no prejudice to allowing Petitioners to request them in this form rather than solely in the prayer for relief. Allowing these causes of action to remain will avoid requiring a further amendment (and another inevitable round of motions) and will not be allowed to expand the scope of issues relevant to the remaining substantive claims (notwithstanding any allegations that may be included in the pleading that might have been relevant to causes of action or theories that are being eliminated by this Order). Finally, the demurrer to individual paragraphs (16, 27-30, 37, and 43) is overruled because a demurrer is not a proper way to attack particular paragraphs as opposed to an entire claim or defense. How Mr. Pongracz's interest in the trust estate is characterized, to the extent it is relevant at all, can be addressed, if necessary, at a later stage in the proceedings. The demurring parties are to file a response to the remaining causes of action within 20 days. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) prior to 4:00 p.m., Wednesday, January 10, 2018. END OF TENTATIVE RULING

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE D J H DATED JULY 24, 1990 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Second Amended Probate Code Sec. 850 Petition
rulings.Rulings: TENTATIVE RULING PART 1 The demurrers are overruled with respect to the First, Second, Third, and Eleventh causes of action for the reasons previously stated in connection with the prior demurrers. The demurrers to the Fourth, Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Tenth causes of action are sustained without leave to amend for lack of standing. Each of these claims is premised on injury allegedly done to decedent James Pongracz, and any recovery would necessarily go to his estate, not to the trust. The substantive rights asserted are designed to protect the decedent's interests-and, therefore, the interests of his heirs and beneficiaries. Those rights are not a mechanism for those adverse to decedent's interests to take property away from decedent or his estate and do not confer standing on strangers to the estate seeking to do so. It remains the case that Petitioners have pleaded no facts that would make them heirs or beneficiaries of Mr. Pongracz's decedent's estate or that would otherwise allow them to share in any recovery on Mr. Pongracz's individual behalf. That fundamental fact is not altered by the estate administrator's decision not to pursue claims on behalf of the estate. Petitioners are not interested parties under any reasonable interpretation of any applicable law and the pleaded facts. Unlike the decedent's granddaughter in Estate of Lowrie (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 220, Petitioners are not even residual beneficiaries of decedent's estate and can claim no potential benefit from any recovery that would go to that estate notwithstanding their uncontested rights as beneficiaries of the trust. Additionally, Petitioners do fit within the exception to standing set out in Welfare and Institutions Code section 15657.3(d)(1)(C) as "a person who has a claim against the estate and who is not an heir or beneficiary of the decedent's estate" (emphasis added) because, although they do not have a creditor's claim, they have made a claim that property titled in decedent's name and claimed by the estate does not belong to the decedent's estate, and although they are beneficiaries of the trust, they are not beneficiaries of the decedent's estate. Whatever rights Petitioners may have to the property at issue will be resolved fully and protected through their 850 petition, in which they will be free to assert whatever relevant bases they believe there are for determining that issue. If the property is determined to belong to the trust, Petitioners will get their full share. If the property belongs to decedent's estate, they will not be entitled to any portion of it. Petitioners have had ample opportunity to try to plead facts that would give them standing to assert these claims, and they have suggested no other facts that could be pleaded to avoid this result. TENTATIVE RULING CONTINUED IN PART 2 (See second line for this demurrer)
